questions.question: Explain the concept of Moving Average in trend prediction. Differentiate between Simple Moving Average and Weighted Moving Average.
keyPoints: Moving Average is a smoothing technique that averages recent data points to identify trends
Simple Moving Average (SMA) assigns equal weight to all points in the window
Weighted Moving Average (WMA) assigns higher weights to recent data
WMA responds faster to recent changes than SMA
Example with calculation showing both methods

questions.question: Describe the four components of a Time Series. Give one example of each from a business context.
keyPoints: Trend: Long-term increase or decrease (e.g., annual revenue growth)
Seasonality: Regular periodic fluctuations (e.g., ice cream sales in summer)
Cyclical: Business cycle fluctuations (e.g., economic recessions every 7-10 years)
Random/Irregular: Unpredictable variations (e.g., sudden supply chain disruption)
Each component contributes to overall time series behavior

questions.question: What is Exponential Smoothing? Explain the role of the smoothing constant Alpha (α) in determining forecast accuracy.
keyPoints: Exponential Smoothing assigns exponentially decreasing weights to older observations
Alpha (α) ranges between 0 and 1
High α (close to 1): More weight to recent data, responsive but volatile
Low α (close to 0): More weight to older data, smoother but slower to react
Choice of α balances responsiveness vs stability

questions.question: Explain Linear Regression method for trend prediction. How do you interpret the slope coefficient in the trend equation?
keyPoints: Linear Regression fits a straight line: y = mx + c
m = slope coefficient indicating rate of change
Positive slope: Upward/increasing trend
Negative slope: Downward/decreasing trend
Slope value indicates unit change in y per unit change in time
c = y-intercept (baseline value)

questions.question: Define forecasting accuracy metrics. Explain Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) with its formula and interpretation.
keyPoints: Metrics measure how close forecasts are to actual values
MAPE = (1/n) × Σ|Actual - Forecast| / Actual × 100%
Lower MAPE indicates better forecast accuracy
MAPE < 10% = Excellent, 10-20% = Good, 20-30% = Fair, >30% = Poor
Useful for comparing different forecasting methods

questions.question: What is the difference between Trend and Seasonality? How does deseasonalization help in trend analysis?
keyPoints: Trend: Long-term directional movement (increasing/decreasing)
Seasonality: Short-term recurring patterns at fixed intervals
Trend shows direction; Seasonality shows recurring patterns
Deseasonalization removes seasonal effects to see underlying trend
Helps in comparing data across different time periods accurately

questions.question: Explain the Naive Forecast method and its limitations. Why is it used as a baseline for comparison?
keyPoints: Naive Forecast: Next period forecast equals current period actual value
Simple to implement and understand
Main limitation: Assumes no change, cannot capture trends or patterns
Used as baseline because it's the simplest prediction method
Other methods should outperform naive to justify complexity
